7 min read
Just now

--

The fear that it makes you lazy is half right. The uncomfortable half is which people it makes lazy.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

royalty-free digital illustrationA few weeks ago I caught myself doing something that made me pause.
I had asked Claude a question, glanced at the answer, and used it without really reading it. Not a hard question. Something I could have worked out myself if I had sat with it for a minute. I did not sit with it for a minute. I took the answer and moved on, and for a second I felt the thing everyone warns you about. That small, uneasy sense that I had just skipped a step my brain was supposed to do.
That feeling has a whole genre of articles behind it now. AI is making us lazy. AI is rotting our thinking. We are outsourcing our minds and we will not get them back. And there is now a real study people point to, so it is not just vibes anymore.
I want to take that fear seriously, because part of it is true. But only part. And the part that is true is not the part people think.
Quick note before I go on: if you want the practical version of using AI without going soft in the head, I send one Claude workflow a week, and a free 10-prompt sampler lands in your inbox the moment you join. You can grab it here: https://joshdubowsky.beehiiv.com. Otherwise, here is what I mean.
The study everyone is quoting
In 2025, MIT’s Media Lab published a paper with a title built for headlines: “Your Brain on ChatGPT.” Researchers put people in three groups to write essays. One group used an AI assistant, one used a search engine, one used nothing but their own head. They measured brain activity with EEG the whole time.
The AI group showed the weakest brain connectivity of the three. The head-only group showed the strongest. The researchers called the gap “cognitive debt,” the mental effort you quietly skip now and pay for later. If you wanted proof that AI dulls you, that is the sentence you would frame.
So the fear is not made up. Lean on AI to do your thinking and your thinking gets quieter. I felt a small version of that myself with one lazy question. The study is real and I am not going to wave it away.
But read one more line of it.
The part that did not make the headlines
The same study had a fourth group. People who wrote the essay with their own brain first, and only then brought AI in. That group stayed sharp. Their engagement did not collapse. And the people who had leaned on AI the whole time, when they were finally asked to work without it, struggled to.
So the study is not really about AI. It is about order and effort. It measured what happens when you let the machine do the thinking instead of you. The people who thought first and used AI second were fine. The tool did not lower their engagement. Handing over the thinking did.
That is the whole thing in one sentence. AI does not replace your brain. Skipping the part where you use it replaces your brain. Those are not the same, and almost every “AI makes you lazy” argument treats them as if they are.
We have done this before
This is not the first tool people were sure would hollow us out.
Calculators were going to end mental arithmetic. They did, a bit. Most of us cannot do long division on paper anymore. But no mathematician got worse at maths because of a calculator. It freed them from the boring part so they could spend their attention on the hard part. Same tool, opposite result, depending entirely on who was holding it and what they used it for.
Satnav is the sharper example. There is good evidence that leaning on turn-by-turn directions weakens your sense of where you are, while people who navigate actively keep that skill. The London taxi drivers who learned the whole city by heart famously grew the part of the brain that handles spatial memory. Nobody blames the map for that difference. The map is the same. The question is whether you are following it half asleep or actually building a picture of the city.
AI is that, turned up. It is the most powerful “let me do that for you” tool we have ever had, which means it is also the most powerful way to stop thinking if that is what you want. The tool does not decide. You do, every time you use it.
The line that decides which way it goes
Here is the distinction I use, and it is the whole game.
There are two things you can hand to AI. You can hand it the drudgery, the formatting, the first rough draft, the boring lookup, the tidying. Or you can hand it the judgment, the deciding, the understanding, the actual thinking about what is true and what matters.
Give it the drudgery and your brain is freed for the part that needs you. Give it the judgment and your brain goes quiet, and stays quiet, and the study is right about you.
Most people never draw that line. They use AI for everything or they fear it for everything. The skill, the entire skill, is knowing which of the two you are handing over in any given moment.
Three ways to use it that make you think more, not less
The trick is to use AI in a way that forces your own brain to show up. These three do that. They are not “give me the answer” prompts. They are “make me think harder” prompts. Copy them, fill in the brackets, and notice that you cannot use any of them on autopilot.
Make it argue with you instead of agree with you:
I am going to tell you what I think about something, and I want you to push back, not agree.What I believe: [YOUR POSITION]
Why I believe it: [YOUR REASONING]Now take the strongest opposing case seriously. Where is my reasoning weakest. What am I not accounting for. Do not be polite about it, be useful. I want to find the holes now, not later.Make it check your thinking instead of doing it for you:
I have already worked out how I want to handle this. I do not want you to redo it. I want you to pressure test it.The situation: [WHAT IS GOING ON]
My plan: [WHAT I HAVE DECIDED TO DO]
My reasoning: [WHY]Tell me where this could go wrong, what I am assuming without checking, and the one question I should answer before I commit. Then stop. I will do the deciding.Make it teach you instead of replace you:
I want to actually understand this, not just get an answer I paste and forget.The thing I want to understand: [TOPIC]
Explain it simply, then ask me a question to check I followed. If I get it wrong, do not just correct me, show me where my thinking went off. We go one step at a time until I could explain it to someone else.None of those let you skip the thinking. They make you bring it. That is the difference between AI that sharpens you and AI that dulls you, and it is entirely in how you ask.
If prompts like these are the kind of thing you want more of, the full library is 200+ built the same way, sorted into 10 categories from money and writing to decisions and study. Copy-paste ready, $7 once, no subscription: The Complete Claude Prompt Library. There is a free sample on the page so you can see the format first.
Skip this if
A few honest disclaimers.
Skip this if you use AI to write things you then hand over as your own understanding, at school or at work. The study is talking directly to you, and it is not wrong. That is the use that dulls you.
Skip this if you were hoping I would tell you AI has no downside. It has a real one. The downside is just a choice you make, not a property of the tool.
Skip this if you want the machine to run your life so you can stop thinking. That is available, and this article is the argument against taking it.
Skip this if you came for coding or developer workflows. This is about the ordinary chat window and an ordinary week.
The honest takeaway
The people worried that AI will replace your brain are picturing the worst way to use it, and calling that the only way. Used that way, they are right, and there is a brain scan to prove it.
But the tool does not make that choice for you. Hand it the drudgery and keep the judgment, and you do not get lazier. You get to spend your actual thinking on the things that are worth thinking about, instead of the busywork that was never the point.
AI is not replacing your brain. It is holding up a mirror and asking whether you brought it. That is a harder question than “is this tool making me lazy,” because the answer is not about the tool. It is about you, and it is different every time you open the thing.
Bring your brain. Hand over the boring part. Keep the part that is actually you.
